title: The Death of the App Store Model
subtitle: The curated marketplace that redefined software distribution is becoming the very constraint it was designed to eliminate. What comes next will be more radical than what came before.

body.content: In 2008, the App Store was a revelation. A single place where anyone could distribute software to millions of people. No retail deals, no shelf space negotiations, no physical media. For developers, it was liberation. For users, it was simplicity. For Apple, it was a masterstroke of ecosystem control that would generate hundreds of billions in revenue.

Eighteen years later, the App Store model is dying. Not because regulators are forcing it open, though they are. Not because developers are revolting against the 30% commission, though they are. The App Store model is dying because the fundamental concept of a 'downloadable application' is becoming obsolete. And when the thing you are selling becomes obsolete, the store that sells it follows.
The App Was Always a Compromise

We have forgotten this, but the native app was always a workaround for a limitation, not a feature in itself. The web in 2007 was too slow and too limited to deliver the experiences that mobile hardware could support. So Apple created an SDK, a distribution mechanism, and a payment system, and told developers: build native, and we will handle the rest.

The result was extraordinary. An explosion of creativity and functionality that transformed the smartphone from a communication device into a general-purpose computer. But it also created a dependency that was never supposed to be permanent. Developers became dependent on Apple's distribution. Users became dependent on Apple's curation. And Apple became dependent on the 30% commission that flowed from both.

The compromises accumulated. Discovery became impossible — with millions of apps, finding anything required external search engines, which defeated the purpose of a curated store. Review times created bottlenecks. Guidelines became Byzantine. The 30% cut, originally justified by the value of distribution and payment processing, began to look like rent-seeking as developers built their own audiences and payment relationships.
AI Dissolves the App Boundary

The real threat to the App Store is not regulatory or competitive. It is architectural. AI agents do not need apps. When an AI assistant can book your restaurant, manage your calendar, draft your emails, and control your smart home, it does not need to open a separate application for each of those tasks. It needs APIs, services, and permissions — but it does not need a user-facing application with an icon on your home screen.

Think about what happens when you ask an AI to 'find me a good Italian restaurant nearby and book a table for two at seven.' In the app model, this requires you to open a restaurant app, search, filter, browse reviews, select a restaurant, open a reservation app, enter your details, select a time, and confirm. In the AI model, you speak a sentence and it is done. The AI contacted the restaurant's booking API directly. No app was opened. No App Store was involved.

Multiply this across every task in your day, and the app model does not just become less convenient. It becomes nonsensical. Why would you maintain hundreds of installed applications when an intelligent agent can access the underlying services directly? The app was always an interface between you and a service. AI is a better interface.
The Platform Paradox

This creates an existential problem for the platform companies, and they know it. Apple's App Store generates an estimated $85 billion in annual revenue. Google Play is not far behind. These are not line items that can be gracefully deprecated. They are load-bearing pillars of two of the most valuable companies in human history.

The paradox is that the same companies are also the best positioned to build the AI layer that replaces their own app stores. Apple's on-device AI processing, Google's cloud AI infrastructure — these are the foundations of the post-app world. But building that world means cannibalizing the store that funds it. This is the classic innovator's dilemma, and it is being played out at a scale that would have made Clayton Christensen dizzy.
What Replaces the Store

The App Store will not disappear overnight. It will erode, the way physical retail eroded — slowly at first, then suddenly. The replacement will not be a better store. It will be a service layer: a marketplace of capabilities rather than applications. Instead of downloading a weather app, your AI will subscribe to a weather data service. Instead of installing a banking app, your AI will connect to your bank's API. Instead of browsing a store full of icons, you will simply tell your AI what you need, and it will find, connect, and orchestrate the services required.

In this model, the value shifts from distribution and curation — which the App Store provides — to trust and orchestration — which the AI layer provides. The company that controls the AI layer controls which services get used, how they get paid, and what cut the platform takes. It is the same economic structure, but the gatekeeping moves from a storefront to an intelligence layer.
The Experience Imperative

The companies that survive this transition will be the ones that understand a fundamental truth: people never wanted apps. They wanted outcomes. The app was simply the best available mechanism for achieving outcomes on a touchscreen computer. When a better mechanism arrives — and AI is that mechanism — the app will retreat to the margins, surviving only for experiences that are genuinely better as dedicated interfaces: games, creative tools, immersive media.

The rest — the utility apps, the single-purpose tools, the services disguised as software — will dissolve into the AI layer. And the App Store, that revolutionary marketplace that once liberated software from the tyranny of physical distribution, will complete its arc from liberation to institution to constraint. It will become what it replaced: an artifact of a previous era's assumptions about how humans and software should interact.

The irony is poetic. The App Store model succeeded because it simplified the relationship between users and software. It will fail because something even simpler has arrived. The best products always contain the seeds of their own obsolescence. The question is whether the companies that built them have the courage to plant what comes next.
